name: Ndengei
description: Ndengei is a primordial serpent or snake deity in Fijian mythology, considered the creator of the world and the judge of souls in the afterlife. He dwells in a vast cave in the Nakauvadra mountain range, specifically in the western part of Viti Levu, Fiji's largest island. Ndengei is depicted as having a body that is part snake and part rock, reflecting his connection to both the earth and the underworld. He is not actively worshipped with sacrifices or offerings in the traditional sense, but is highly revered and feared. His movements are believed to cause earthquakes, and his moods influence the success or failure of crops and fishing. Ndengei judges the souls of the dead as they pass through the afterlife, deciding their fate based on their actions in life. Those deemed worthy are allowed to enter paradise, while the wicked are punished. He is a powerful and enigmatic figure, embodying the creative and destructive forces of nature, and holding significant sway over the lives and destinies of the Fijian people. Ndengei is also sometimes associated with fertility and the continuation of life, linking him to the cyclical nature of existence. His presence is a constant reminder of the power of the natural world and the importance of living a righteous life.
subtitle: Creator Serpent and Judge of Souls

keyMyths.title: The Creation of Fiji
keyMyths.description: Ndengei is said to have created the islands of Fiji through his movements and actions, shaping the land and seas. His body is intertwined with the very fabric of the Fijian landscape.

keyMyths.title: Judgment in the Cave
keyMyths.description: After death, souls travel to Ndengei's cave, where he questions them about their lives and determines their fate. The righteous are rewarded, while the wicked are punished in various ways.

keyMyths.title: Ndengei and Earthquakes
keyMyths.description: Whenever Ndengei shifts or moves within his cave, the earth trembles, causing earthquakes. This connection highlights his power over the natural world.

sources.author: Basil Thomson
